source: Gaze Glass — A Family of Mortals (2).docx
heading: 5 Jermaine
paragraphs: Marok chewed on musky Fox nip with pink eyes and hopped from cloud to cloud with glee.

Below, Jermaine panicked. The mortal was too proud to admit—even to himself—that being in India wasn’t as exciting as he thought it would be; it was a series of traumatic events that made him miss home. Although he was grateful Rashid’s family had invited him to join them, he began to regret his decision. Some of the food was too spicy to eat. Acid reflux irritated his chest and throat at night. The mosquitoes left itchy, giant bumps on his ankles and arms. The humidity and heat left his arms feeling sticky. Locals watched him and whispered. He couldn’t understand them, and that made him even more uncomfortable.
“He was literally right here!” Jermaine exclaimed. “Where did that cow go?”

He hadn’t noticed the two girls emerging from behind a decaying banyan tree. Rashid’s twenty-year-old curvy sisters, Priya and Sonam, were dressed in matching sleeveless emerald dresses. Between them, the calf marched cheerfully, brushing its head against Priya’s leg.

“We’ve been searching for the two of you for hours!” Priya snapped as they got closer. She drew her long, raven hair through a fraying hair band, exposing a series of gold piercings along her ear.  “I’m sorry! We found this baby cow and—" Rashid started to say. Priya snapped her fingers in the air above Jermaine’s nose. “And as for you! Do you know what happens to foreigners in India?” she asked, tapping her right foot on the ground. A mosquito landed on Jermaine’s nose. He shook his face and growled. Sonam watched on apathetically, wiping her oily forehead. Unlike her twin, Sonam had pimples and acne scars and wore no jewelry, but a silver, tear-shaped Bindi glistened between her bushy black eyebrows. Howling winds swept through the jungle as dark, storm clouds formed above.
“You could have been bit by a cobra or killed by a witch or abducted by a cannibal tribe!” Priya tilted her head and scolded them. “And where is Sahil? Did you two find him?”
Sonam chuckled and cracked her knuckles; the sound sent a shiver down Jermaine’s spine.
“You think that’s funny?” Priya asked, turning to her.
“No, you’re just an idiot. The Aghori tribe is nowhere near here,” Sonam explained.
Jermaine froze. “Wait. You ain’t playing?” A mosquito hovered near his nose, but he didn’t care. “Their tribe eats from a funeral pyre. It’s a religious custom,” Sonam said confidently.
Jermaine chewed on his bottom lip before scratching the back of his head. Sonam wiggled her fingers and smirked. “Afraid of cannibals? Good. You should be,” Sonam warned.

The calf nudged the side of his face against Priya’s arm. The poor baby felt sad and missed its mother. “What do we do with you? You are cute,” Priya said and looked in every direction and frowned at weeping Banyan trees and petrified bushes. A gray thundercloud loomed over the four.  “Drop it off at the closest temple. They’ll find its owner!” Sonam suggested.
“Grandmother told us to take the cow to its owner by the old mosque.” Rashid said.
“Are you an idiot?” Sonam asked, “Mom will kill us!”
“She won’t if she doesn’t find out!” Priya smiled.
“I’m not getting involved!” Sonam complained.
“No one asked you to!” Rashid growled at Sonam.
“And you’re a waste of space!” Sonam spat back.
Rashid watched Sonam place the palm of her hand on the calf’s back, her voice joined new thunder when the calf bellowed. Everyone laughed after she shrieked.
“Wow. Even cows don’t want to be friends with you!” Rashid said and laughed.

Sonam’s nostrils flared and her chest rose. A bright white light blinded the four and thunder shook the trees. Sonam stepped forward and put her hands on her hips and smirked. “I’m sure you know a lot about friendship. Don’t you? You seem to be really close with your friends. Some closer than others,” Sonam said in a singsong voice. Rashid took a step back and swallowed.
“What are you talking about?” Priya asked.

Sonam leaned forward, her smile growing wider. Cool wind scooped leaves from the dirt and fluttered around Jermaine’s muscular legs. “Dad borrowed my copy of Lust and Grief. He never gave it back and I needed a copy for a report,” Sonam said slowly, emphasizing every syllable to trigger Rashid’s anxiety.

Rashid went pale. Jermaine could feel his anxiety. He had never seen Rashid like this before, and he didn’t like how Sonam made him feel. But he was conflicted. If it weren’t for her, he wouldn’t have graduated. After school, she’d tutor him. There were moments when she made him feel like an idiot, but he began to understand her. Over time, he developed strong, romantic feelings towards her. But he’d never tell her how he felt since she was his best friend’s sister.
“A book? Really?” Jermaine asked and stepped forward, dead leaves crunching under his feet.
“Stay out of this,” Sonam growled, snapping her fingers at him.

Jermaine squeezed his lips together and waited. He knew Rashid well or so he thought. If she divulged one of Rashid’s secrets, he’d defend him. Jermaine knew Rashid well and wondered which of his secrets Sonam harbored. “I found a few photos hidden in the book—” Sonam started. “Stop!” Rashid shouted.
Priya’s eyes darted between her siblings.
“Rashid was kissing one of his friends,” Sonam teased. “It’s so scandalous!”
Sweat trickled down the back of Rashid's neck and Jermaine filled his lungs with cool air.
“Please,” Rashid begged, tears forming under his eyes.
Jermaine laughed and put his arm around Rashid’s sweaty neck. He was relieved.
“Wow! You found a photo of Rashid kissing Tamara. Big deal!” Jermaine said. “We know he’s had a crush on her for a while … it’s old news!”
Sonam laughed and eyed her brother’s frown and smiled with her eyes.
“Tamara? I didn’t see her in any of those photos,” Sonam said. “Not a single one!”
Tears rolled down Rashid’s cheeks and he began to tremble.
“That’s enough!” Priya warned.
Sonam wiggled her fingers and laughed. “Rashid. Tell me. What would mom think if she knew you were kissing boys?” Sonam asked.
